name: Simple GET — baseline
description: The simplest possible test. No variables, no body. Use this to establish a throughput and latency baseline for any endpoint.

name: Authenticated GET
description: Adds an Authorization header. The auth_token value is passed verbatim — include the scheme (Bearer, Token, Basic, etc.).

name: POST with a static body
description: Sends the same JSON body with every request. Good for testing write endpoints when you don't need varied data.

name: Sequential IDs in URL — static strategy
description: Uses a static variable. Every request hits the same resource. Useful when you want to test one specific record under load.

name: Paginated endpoint — sequential strategy
description: Cycles through pages 1-20 in order. Sequential wraps back to startValue after reaching endValue, so with 200 requests and 20 pages each page gets hit ~10 times.

name: Random user lookup — random strategy
description: Fetches a random user ID between 1 and 1000 on every request. Simulates a realistic read pattern that avoids cache warming on a single record.

name: Create resource with unique ID — uuid strategy
description: Generates a new UUID for every request. Use this when the endpoint requires a unique identifier per call, such as idempotency keys or client-generated IDs.

name: Ingest event with current timestamp — timestamp strategy
description: Stamps each request with the current time in RFC3339 format. Useful for event ingestion, audit log, or time-series endpoints.

name: Generated usernames — template strategy
description: Builds a string from a template using the built-in {{$index}} and {{$random}} placeholders. Produces values like user_0, user_1, user_2 etc. No variable definition needed for $index and $random — they work anywhere.

name: Full example — multiple variables combined
description: Shows all variable types working together in one test. Mix and match strategies freely — each variable is resolved independently per request.
